Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.
==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[Muromachi period]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{Cite journal |last=Hisao |first=Maye |date=1997 |title=Theorizing About the Origins of the Tokonoma |journal=Chanoyu Quarterly |issue=86 |pages=22–39 |issn=0009-1537 |oclc=4044546}}
==External links== * {{Commons category-inline}}
{{Japanese architectural elements}}]] ]]

revision.text:
{{short description|Alcove in Japanese reception rooms}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Kannonin Tottori16s4470.jpg|thumb|A {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} with a [[:en:Hanging scroll|Hanging scroll]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement]] [[File:Tokonoma (床の間).jpg|thumb|Detailed view of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and aspects of a Japanese room]] [[File:Murotsu Museum of Folklore08s5s4272.jpg|thumb|View from the side of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}]] [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto29s5s4200.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Tokonoma}} at [[Tenryū-ji]]]]
A {{nihongo||床の間|'''tokonoma'''}},<ref>''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'', Kenkyusha Limited, {{ISBN|4-7674-2015-6}}</ref> or simply {{nihongo||床|'''toko'''}},<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for ''tokonoma.''</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu. Iguchi Kaisen, et al., supv. eds. (Kyoto: Tankosha, 1986 10th ed.) entry for ''Toko.''</ref> is a recessed space in a Japanese-style reception room, in which items for artistic appreciation are displayed. In English, a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} could be called an [[:en:Alcove (architecture)|alcove]].
==History== There are two theories about the predecessor of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}: the first is that it derives from the room structure of the {{transliteration|ja|shinden-zukuri}}, which flourished in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) and declined in the [[Muromachi period]] (1336–1573); the second is that it derives from the room structure of [[Zen]] monasteries in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] (1185–1333). In the room of the monastery, there was a board called {{nihongo||押板|oshiita}} which displayed Buddhist altar fittings such as candlesticks, incense burners and vases. On the wall behind {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} was a hanging scroll with a Buddhist theme. The second theory is that the {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} and the back wall developed into a {{transliteration|ja|[[shoin-zukuri]]}}-style {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} in the Muromachi period.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20210411010010/https://wafujyutaku.jp/archives/alcove-cat/seishinsei Tokonoma.] Shinken press.</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu, entry for ''Toko''</ref>
In {{transliteration|ja|shoin-zukuri}}, an architectural style developed in the Muromachi period, {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} came to be used as room decoration, and the owner of the house sat in front of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} decorated with various things to meet guests. However, in the case of important guests, the householder, in deference to them, had them sit in front of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/save/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BA%8A%E3%81%AE%E9%96%93-105027 Tokonoma.] Kotobank</ref>
==Characteristics== The items typically displayed in a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} are calligraphic or pictorial [[:en:Hanging scroll|scrolls]] and an {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement. [[:en:Bonsai|Bonsai]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[okimono]]}} are also common—although traditionally, bonsai were not considered worthy for a place of such respect. The {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and its contents are essential elements of traditional Japanese interior decoration. The [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] {{transliteration|ja|toko}} (床) literally means "floor" or "bed"; {{transliteration|ja|ma}} (間) means "space" or "room". When seating guests in a Japanese-style room, the correct [[:en:Japanese etiquette|etiquette]] is to seat the most important guest closest to the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} as this is in the location furthest from the entrance, a location called the {{transliteration|ja|kamiza}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Vardaman |first1=James M. |title=Japanese etiquette today : a guide to business & social customs |date=1994 |publisher=C.E. Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.
==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[Muromachi period]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{Cite journal |last=Hisao |first=Maye |date=1997 |title=Theorizing About the Origins of the Tokonoma |journal=Chanoyu Quarterly |issue=86 |pages=22–39 |issn=0009-1537 |oclc=4044546}}

{{Japanese architectural elements}}]] ]]

(Kyoto: Tankosha, 1986 10th ed.) entry for ''Toko.''</ref> is a recessed space in a Japanese-style reception room, in which items for artistic appreciation are displayed. ==History== There are two theories about the predecessor of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}: the first is that it derives from the room structure of the {{transliteration|ja|shinden-zukuri}}, which flourished in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) and declined in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1336–1573); the second is that it derives from the room structure of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] monasteries in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] (1185–1333). In the room of the monastery, there was a board called {{nihongo||押板|oshiita}} which displayed Buddhist altar fittings such as candlesticks, incense burners and vases. On the wall behind {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} was a hanging scroll with a Buddhist theme. The second theory is that the {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} and the back wall developed into a {{transliteration|ja|[[shoin-zukuri]]}}-style {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} in the Muromachi period.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20210411010010/https://wafujyutaku.jp/archives/alcove-cat/seishinsei Tokonoma.] Shinken press.</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu, entry for ''Toko''</ref>
In {{transliteration|ja|shoin-zukuri}}, an architectural style developed in the Muromachi period, {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} came to be used as room decoration, and the owner of the house sat in front of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} decorated with various things to meet guests. However, in the case of important guests, the householder, in deference to them, had them sit in front of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/save/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BA%8A%E3%81%AE%E9%96%93-105027 Tokonoma.] Kotobank</ref>
==Characteristics== The items typically displayed in a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} are calligraphic or pictorial [[:en:Hanging scroll|scrolls]] and an {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement. [[:en:Bonsai|Bonsai]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Okimono|Okimono]]}} are also common—although traditionally, bonsai were not considered worthy for a place of such respect. The {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and its contents are essential elements of traditional Japanese interior decoration. The [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] {{transliteration|ja|toko}} (床) literally means "floor" or "bed"; {{transliteration|ja|ma}} (間) means "space" or "room". When seating guests in a Japanese-style room, the correct [[:en:Japanese etiquette|etiquette]] is to seat the most important guest closest to the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} as this is in the location furthest from the entrance, a location called the {{transliteration|ja|kamiza}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Vardaman |first1=James M. |title=Japanese etiquette today : a guide to business & social customs |date=1994 |publisher=C.E. Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.
==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{Cite journal |last=Hisao |first=Maye |date=1997 |title=Theorizing About the Origins of the Tokonoma |journal=Chanoyu Quarterly |issue=86 |pages=22–39 |issn=0009-1537 |oclc=4044546}}

Iguchi Kaisen, et al., supv. eds. However, in the case of important guests, the householder, in deference to them, had them sit in front of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/save/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BA%8A%E3%81%AE%E9%96%93-105027 Tokonoma.] Kotobank</ref>

[[:en:Bonsai|Bonsai]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Okimono|Okimono]]}} are also common—although traditionally, bonsai were not considered worthy for a place of such respect. Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:توكونوما]][[be:Токанама]][[ca:Tokonoma]][[commons:Category:Tokonoma]][[cs:Tokonoma]][[de:Tokonoma]][[en:Tokonoma]][[eo:Tokonomo]][[es:Tokonoma]][[fa:تماشاگه]][[fr:Tokonoma]][[he:טוקונומה]][[id:Tokonoma]][[it:Tokonoma]][[ja:床の間]][[ko:도코노마]][[nl:Tokonoma]][[pl:Tokonoma]][[pt:Tokonoma]][[ru:Токонома]][[sl:Tokonoma]][[tr:Tokonoma]][[uk:Токонома]][[vi:Tokonoma]][[zh:床之間]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q738760]] Wikidata name: tokonoma [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Alcove in Japanese reception rooms}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Kannonin Tottori16s4470.jpg|thumb|A {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} with a [[:en:Hanging scroll|Hanging scroll]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement]] [[File:Tokonoma (床の間).jpg|thumb|Detailed view of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and aspects of a Japanese room]] [[File:Murotsu Museum of Folklore08s5s4272.jpg|thumb|View from the side of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}]] [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto29s5s4200.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Tokonoma}} at [[Tenryū-ji]]]]
A {{nihongo||床の間|'''tokonoma'''}},<ref>''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'', Kenkyusha Limited, {{ISBN|4-7674-2015-6}}</ref> or simply {{nihongo||床|'''toko'''}},<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for ''tokonoma.''</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu. Iguchi Kaisen, et al., supv. eds. (Kyoto: Tankosha, 1986 10th ed.) entry for ''Toko.''</ref> is a recessed space in a Japanese-style reception room, in which items for artistic appreciation are displayed. In English, a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} could be called an [[:en:Alcove (architecture)|alcove]].
==History== There are two theories about the predecessor of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}: the first is that it derives from the room structure of the {{transliteration|ja|shinden-zukuri}}, which flourished in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) and declined in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1336–1573); the second is that it derives from the room structure of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] monasteries in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] (1185–1333). In the room of the monastery, there was a board called {{nihongo||押板|oshiita}} which displayed Buddhist altar fittings such as candlesticks, incense burners and vases. On the wall behind {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} was a hanging scroll with a Buddhist theme. The second theory is that the {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} and the back wall developed into a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]]}}-style {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} in the Muromachi period.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20210411010010/https://wafujyutaku.jp/archives/alcove-cat/seishinsei Tokonoma.] Shinken press.</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu, entry for ''Toko''</ref>
In {{transliteration|ja|shoin-zukuri}}, an architectural style developed in the Muromachi period, {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} came to be used as room decoration, and the owner of the house sat in front of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} decorated with various things to meet guests. However, in the case of important guests, the householder, in deference to them, had them sit in front of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/save/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BA%8A%E3%81%AE%E9%96%93-105027 Tokonoma.] Kotobank</ref>
==Characteristics== The items typically displayed in a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} are calligraphic or pictorial [[:en:Hanging scroll|scrolls]] and an {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement. [[:en:Bonsai|Bonsai]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Okimono|Okimono]]}} are also common—although traditionally, bonsai were not considered worthy for a place of such respect. The {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and its contents are essential elements of traditional Japanese interior decoration. The [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] {{transliteration|ja|toko}} (床) literally means "floor" or "bed"; {{transliteration|ja|ma}} (間) means "space" or "room". When seating guests in a Japanese-style room, the correct [[:en:Japanese etiquette|etiquette]] is to seat the most important guest closest to the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} as this is in the location furthest from the entrance, a location called the {{transliteration|ja|kamiza}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Vardaman |first1=James M. |title=Japanese etiquette today : a guide to business & social customs |date=1994 |publisher=C.E. Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.
==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{Cite journal |last=Hisao |first=Maye |date=1997 |title=Theorizing About the Origins of the Tokonoma |journal=Chanoyu Quarterly |issue=86 |pages=22–39 |issn=0009-1537 |oclc=4044546}}
==External links== * {{Commons category-inline}}

[[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese home]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]
}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:توكونوما]][[be:Токанама]][[ca:Tokonoma]][[sn:commons:Category:Tokonoma]][[cs:Tokonoma]][[de:Tokonoma]][[en:Tokonoma]][[eo:Tokonomo]][[es:Tokonoma]][[fa:تماشاگه]][[fr:Tokonoma]][[he:טוקונומה]][[id:Tokonoma]][[it:Tokonoma]][[ja:床の間]][[ko:도코노마]][[nl:Tokonoma]][[pl:Tokonoma]][[pt:Tokonoma]][[ru:Токонома]][[sl:Tokonoma]][[tr:Tokonoma]][[uk:Токонома]][[vi:Tokonoma]][[zh:床之間]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q738760]] Wikidata name: tokonoma [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Alcove in Japanese reception rooms}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Kannonin Tottori16s4470.jpg|thumb|A {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} with a [[:en:Hanging scroll|Hanging scroll]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement]] [[File:Tokonoma (床の間).jpg|thumb|Detailed view of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and aspects of a Japanese room]] [[File:Murotsu Museum of Folklore08s5s4272.jpg|thumb|View from the side of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}]] [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto29s5s4200.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Tokonoma}} at [[Tenryū-ji]]]]
A {{nihongo||床の間|'''tokonoma'''}},<ref>''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'', Kenkyusha Limited, {{ISBN|4-7674-2015-6}}</ref> or simply {{nihongo||床|'''toko'''}},<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for ''tokonoma.''</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu. Iguchi Kaisen, et al., supv. eds. (Kyoto: Tankosha, 1986 10th ed.) entry for ''Toko.''</ref> is a recessed space in a Japanese-style reception room, in which items for artistic appreciation are displayed. In English, a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} could be called an [[:en:Alcove (architecture)|alcove]].
==History== There are two theories about the predecessor of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}: the first is that it derives from the room structure of the {{transliteration|ja|shinden-zukuri}}, which flourished in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) and declined in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1336–1573); the second is that it derives from the room structure of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] monasteries in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] (1185–1333). In the room of the monastery, there was a board called {{nihongo||押板|oshiita}} which displayed Buddhist altar fittings such as candlesticks, incense burners and vases. On the wall behind {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} was a hanging scroll with a Buddhist theme. The second theory is that the {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} and the back wall developed into a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]]}}-style {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} in the Muromachi period.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20210411010010/https://wafujyutaku.jp/archives/alcove-cat/seishinsei Tokonoma.] Shinken press.</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu, entry for ''Toko''</ref>
In {{transliteration|ja|shoin-zukuri}}, an architectural style developed in the Muromachi period, {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} came to be used as room decoration, and the owner of the house sat in front of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} decorated with various things to meet guests. However, in the case of important guests, the householder, in deference to them, had them sit in front of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/save/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BA%8A%E3%81%AE%E9%96%93-105027 Tokonoma.] Kotobank</ref>
==Characteristics== The items typically displayed in a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} are calligraphic or pictorial [[:en:Hanging scroll|scrolls]] and an {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement. [[:en:Bonsai|Bonsai]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Okimono|Okimono]]}} are also common—although traditionally, bonsai were not considered worthy for a place of such respect. The {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and its contents are essential elements of traditional Japanese interior decoration. The [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] {{transliteration|ja|toko}} (床) literally means "floor" or "bed"; {{transliteration|ja|ma}} (間) means "space" or "room". When seating guests in a Japanese-style room, the correct [[:en:Japanese etiquette|etiquette]] is to seat the most important guest closest to the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} as this is in the location furthest from the entrance, a location called the {{transliteration|ja|kamiza}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Vardaman |first1=James M. |title=Japanese etiquette today : a guide to business & social customs |date=1994 |publisher=C.E. Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.
==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{Cite journal |last=Hisao |first=Maye |date=1997 |title=Theorizing About the Origins of the Tokonoma |journal=Chanoyu Quarterly |issue=86 |pages=22–39 |issn=0009-1537 |oclc=4044546}}
==External links== * {{Commons category-inline}}
{{Japanese architectural elements}}]] {{draft categories|
[[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese home]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]
}}
[[en:Tokonoma]]

A {{nihongo||床の間|'''tokonoma'''}},<ref>''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'', Kenkyusha Limited, {{ISBN|4-7674-2015-6}}</ref> or simply {{nihongo||床|'''toko'''}},<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for ''tokonoma.''</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu. Iguchi Kaisen, et al., supv. eds. (Kyoto: Tankosha, 1986 10th ed.) entry for ''Toko.''</ref> is a recessed space in a Japanese-style reception room, in which items for artistic appreciation are displayed. In English, a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} could be called an [[:en:Alcove (architecture)|alcove]].
==History== There are two theories about the predecessor of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}: the first is that it derives from the room structure of the {{transliteration|ja|shinden-zukuri}}, which flourished in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) and declined in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1336–1573); the second is that it derives from the room structure of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] monasteries in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] (1185–1333). In the room of the monastery, there was a board called {{nihongo||押板|oshiita}} which displayed Buddhist altar fittings such as candlesticks, incense burners and vases. On the wall behind {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} was a hanging scroll with a Buddhist theme. The second theory is that the {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} and the back wall developed into a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]]}}-style {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} in the Muromachi period.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20210411010010/https://wafujyutaku.jp/archives/alcove-cat/seishinsei Tokonoma.] Shinken press.</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu, entry for ''Toko''</ref>
In {{transliteration|ja|shoin-zukuri}}, an architectural style developed in the Muromachi period, {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} came to be used as room decoration, and the owner of the house sat in front of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} decorated with various things to meet guests. However, in the case of important guests, the householder, in deference to them, had them sit in front of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/save/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BA%8A%E3%81%AE%E9%96%93-105027 Tokonoma.] Kotobank</ref>
==Characteristics== The items typically displayed in a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} are calligraphic or pictorial [[:en:Hanging scroll|scrolls]] and an {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement. [[:en:Bonsai|Bonsai]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Okimono|Okimono]]}} are also common—although traditionally, bonsai were not considered worthy for a place of such respect. The {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and its contents are essential elements of traditional Japanese interior decoration. The [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] {{transliteration|ja|toko}} (床) literally means "floor" or "bed"; {{transliteration|ja|ma}} (間) means "space" or "room". When seating guests in a Japanese-style room, the correct [[:en:Japanese etiquette|etiquette]] is to seat the most important guest closest to the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} as this is in the location furthest from the entrance, a location called the {{transliteration|ja|kamiza}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Vardaman |first1=James M. |title=Japanese etiquette today : a guide to business & social customs |date=1994 |publisher=C.E. Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.
==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{Cite journal |last=Hisao |first=Maye |date=1997 |title=Theorizing About the Origins of the Tokonoma |journal=Chanoyu Quarterly |issue=86 |pages=22–39 |issn=0009-1537 |oclc=4044546}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:توكونوما]][[be:Токанама]][[ca:Tokonoma]][[sn:commons:Category:Tokonoma]][[cs:Tokonoma]][[de:Tokonoma]][[en:Tokonoma]][[eo:Tokonomo]][[es:Tokonoma]][[fa:تماشاگه]][[fr:Tokonoma]][[he:טוקונומה]][[id:Tokonoma]][[it:Tokonoma]][[ja:床の間]][[ko:도코노마]][[nl:Tokonoma]][[pl:Tokonoma]][[pt:Tokonoma]][[ru:Токонома]][[sl:Tokonoma]][[tr:Tokonoma]][[uk:Токонома]][[vi:Tokonoma]][[zh:床之間]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q738760]] Wikidata name: tokonoma [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Alcove in Japanese reception rooms}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Kannonin Tottori16s4470.jpg|thumb|A {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} with a [[:en:Hanging scroll|Hanging scroll]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement]] [[File:Tokonoma (床の間).jpg|thumb|Detailed view of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and aspects of a Japanese room]] [[File:Murotsu Museum of Folklore08s5s4272.jpg|thumb|View from the side of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}]] [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto29s5s4200.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Tokonoma}} at [[Tenryū-ji]]]]
A {{nihongo||床の間|'''tokonoma'''}},<ref>''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'', Kenkyusha Limited, {{ISBN|4-7674-2015-6}}</ref> or simply {{nihongo||床|'''toko'''}},<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for ''tokonoma.''</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu. Iguchi Kaisen, et al., supv. eds. (Kyoto: Tankosha, 1986 10th ed.) entry for ''Toko.''</ref> is a recessed space in a Japanese-style reception room, in which items for artistic appreciation are displayed. In English, a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} could be called an [[:en:Alcove (architecture)|alcove]].
==History== There are two theories about the predecessor of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}: the first is that it derives from the room structure of the {{transliteration|ja|shinden-zukuri}}, which flourished in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) and declined in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1336–1573); the second is that it derives from the room structure of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] monasteries in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] (1185–1333). In the room of the monastery, there was a board called {{nihongo||押板|oshiita}} which displayed Buddhist altar fittings such as candlesticks, incense burners and vases. On the wall behind {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} was a hanging scroll with a Buddhist theme. The second theory is that the {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} and the back wall developed into a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]]}}-style {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} in the Muromachi period.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20210411010010/https://wafujyutaku.jp/archives/alcove-cat/seishinsei Tokonoma.] Shinken press.</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu, entry for ''Toko''</ref>
In {{transliteration|ja|shoin-zukuri}}, an architectural style developed in the Muromachi period, {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} came to be used as room decoration, and the owner of the house sat in front of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} decorated with various things to meet guests. However, in the case of important guests, the householder, in deference to them, had them sit in front of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/save/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BA%8A%E3%81%AE%E9%96%93-105027 Tokonoma.] Kotobank</ref>
==Characteristics== The items typically displayed in a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} are calligraphic or pictorial [[:en:Hanging scroll|scrolls]] and an {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement. [[:en:Bonsai|Bonsai]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Okimono|Okimono]]}} are also common—although traditionally, bonsai were not considered worthy for a place of such respect. The {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and its contents are essential elements of traditional Japanese interior decoration. The [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] {{transliteration|ja|toko}} (床) literally means "floor" or "bed"; {{transliteration|ja|ma}} (間) means "space" or "room". When seating guests in a Japanese-style room, the correct [[:en:Japanese etiquette|etiquette]] is to seat the most important guest closest to the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} as this is in the location furthest from the entrance, a location called the {{transliteration|ja|kamiza}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Vardaman |first1=James M. |title=Japanese etiquette today : a guide to business & social customs |date=1994 |publisher=C.E. Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.
==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{Cite journal |last=Hisao |first=Maye |date=1997 |title=Theorizing About the Origins of the Tokonoma |journal=Chanoyu Quarterly |issue=86 |pages=22–39 |issn=0009-1537 |oclc=4044546}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:توكونوما]][[be:Токанама]][[ca:Tokonoma]][[sn:commons:Category:Tokonoma]][[cs:Tokonoma]][[de:Tokonoma]][[en:Tokonoma]][[eo:Tokonomo]][[es:Tokonoma]][[fa:تماشاگه]][[fr:Tokonoma]][[he:טוקונומה]][[id:Tokonoma]][[it:Tokonoma]][[ja:床の間]][[ko:도코노마]][[nl:Tokonoma]][[pl:Tokonoma]][[pt:Tokonoma]][[ru:Токонома]][[sl:Tokonoma]][[tr:Tokonoma]][[uk:Токонома]][[vi:Tokonoma]][[zh:床之間]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q738760]] Wikidata name: tokonoma [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Alcove in Japanese reception rooms}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Kannonin Tottori16s4470.jpg|thumb|A {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} with a [[:en:Hanging scroll|Hanging scroll]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement]] [[File:Tokonoma (床の間).jpg|thumb|Detailed view of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and aspects of a Japanese room]] [[File:Murotsu Museum of Folklore08s5s4272.jpg|thumb|View from the side of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}]] [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto29s5s4200.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Tokonoma}} at [[Tenryū-ji]]]]
A {{nihongo||床の間|'''tokonoma'''}},<ref>''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'', Kenkyusha Limited, {{ISBN|4-7674-2015-6}}</ref> or simply {{nihongo||床|'''toko'''}},<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for ''tokonoma.''</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu. Iguchi Kaisen, et al., supv. eds. (Kyoto: Tankosha, 1986 10th ed.) entry for ''Toko.''</ref> is a recessed space in a Japanese-style reception room, in which items for artistic appreciation are displayed. In English, a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} could be called an [[:en:Alcove (architecture)|alcove]].
==History== There are two theories about the predecessor of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}: the first is that it derives from the room structure of the {{transliteration|ja|shinden-zukuri}}, which flourished in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) and declined in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1336–1573); the second is that it derives from the room structure of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] monasteries in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] (1185–1333). In the room of the monastery, there was a board called {{nihongo||押板|oshiita}} which displayed Buddhist altar fittings such as candlesticks, incense burners and vases. On the wall behind {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} was a hanging scroll with a Buddhist theme. The second theory is that the {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} and the back wall developed into a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]]}}-style {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} in the Muromachi period.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20210411010010/https://wafujyutaku.jp/archives/alcove-cat/seishinsei Tokonoma.] Shinken press.</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu, entry for ''Toko''</ref>
In {{transliteration|ja|shoin-zukuri}}, an architectural style developed in the Muromachi period, {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} came to be used as room decoration, and the owner of the house sat in front of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} decorated with various things to meet guests. However, in the case of important guests, the householder, in deference to them, had them sit in front of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/save/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BA%8A%E3%81%AE%E9%96%93-105027 Tokonoma.] Kotobank</ref>
==Characteristics== The items typically displayed in a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} are calligraphic or pictorial [[:en:Hanging scroll|scrolls]] and an {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement. [[:en:Bonsai|Bonsai]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Okimono|Okimono]]}} are also common—although traditionally, bonsai were not considered worthy for a place of such respect. The {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and its contents are essential elements of traditional Japanese interior decoration. The [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] {{transliteration|ja|toko}} (床) literally means "floor" or "bed"; {{transliteration|ja|ma}} (間) means "space" or "room". When seating guests in a Japanese-style room, the correct [[:en:Japanese etiquette|etiquette]] is to seat the most important guest closest to the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} as this is in the location furthest from the entrance, a location called the {{transliteration|ja|kamiza}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Vardaman |first1=James M. |title=Japanese etiquette today : a guide to business & social customs |date=1994 |publisher=C.E. Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.
==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{Cite journal |last=Hisao |first=Maye |date=1997 |title=Theorizing About the Origins of the Tokonoma |journal=Chanoyu Quarterly |issue=86 |pages=22–39 |issn=0009-1537 |oclc=4044546}}
==External links== * {{Commons category-inline}}

}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:توكونوما]][[be:Токанама]][[ca:Tokonoma]][[sn:commons:Category:Tokonoma]][[cs:Tokonoma]][[de:Tokonoma]][[en:Tokonoma]][[eo:Tokonomo]][[es:Tokonoma]][[fa:تماشاگه]][[fr:Tokonoma]][[he:טוקונומה]][[id:Tokonoma]][[it:Tokonoma]][[ja:床の間]][[ko:도코노마]][[nl:Tokonoma]][[pl:Tokonoma]][[pt:Tokonoma]][[ru:Токонома]][[sl:Tokonoma]][[tr:Tokonoma]][[uk:Токонома]][[vi:Tokonoma]][[zh:床之間]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q738760]] Wikidata name: tokonoma [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Alcove in Japanese reception rooms}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Kannonin Tottori16s4470.jpg|thumb|A {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} with a [[:en:Hanging scroll|Hanging scroll]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement]] [[File:Tokonoma (床の間).jpg|thumb|Detailed view of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and aspects of a Japanese room]] [[File:Murotsu Museum of Folklore08s5s4272.jpg|thumb|View from the side of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}]] [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto29s5s4200.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Tokonoma}} at [[Tenryū-ji]]]]
A {{nihongo||床の間|'''tokonoma'''}},<ref>''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'', Kenkyusha Limited, {{ISBN|4-7674-2015-6}}</ref> or simply {{nihongo||床|'''toko'''}},<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for ''tokonoma.''</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu. Iguchi Kaisen, et al., supv. eds. (Kyoto: Tankosha, 1986 10th ed.) entry for ''Toko.''</ref> is a recessed space in a Japanese-style reception room, in which items for artistic appreciation are displayed. In English, a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} could be called an [[:en:Alcove (architecture)|alcove]].
==History== There are two theories about the predecessor of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}: the first is that it derives from the room structure of the {{transliteration|ja|shinden-zukuri}}, which flourished in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) and declined in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1336–1573); the second is that it derives from the room structure of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] monasteries in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] (1185–1333). In the room of the monastery, there was a board called {{nihongo||押板|oshiita}} which displayed Buddhist altar fittings such as candlesticks, incense burners and vases. On the wall behind {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} was a hanging scroll with a Buddhist theme. The second theory is that the {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} and the back wall developed into a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]]}}-style {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} in the Muromachi period.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20210411010010/https://wafujyutaku.jp/archives/alcove-cat/seishinsei Tokonoma.] Shinken press.</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu, entry for ''Toko''</ref>
In {{transliteration|ja|shoin-zukuri}}, an architectural style developed in the Muromachi period, {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} came to be used as room decoration, and the owner of the house sat in front of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} decorated with various things to meet guests. However, in the case of important guests, the householder, in deference to them, had them sit in front of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/save/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BA%8A%E3%81%AE%E9%96%93-105027 Tokonoma.] Kotobank</ref>
==Characteristics== The items typically displayed in a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} are calligraphic or pictorial [[:en:Hanging scroll|scrolls]] and an {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement. [[:en:Bonsai|Bonsai]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Okimono|Okimono]]}} are also common—although traditionally, bonsai were not considered worthy for a place of such respect. The {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and its contents are essential elements of traditional Japanese interior decoration. The [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] {{transliteration|ja|toko}} (床) literally means "floor" or "bed"; {{transliteration|ja|ma}} (間) means "space" or "room". When seating guests in a Japanese-style room, the correct [[:en:Japanese etiquette|etiquette]] is to seat the most important guest closest to the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} as this is in the location furthest from the entrance, a location called the {{transliteration|ja|kamiza}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Vardaman |first1=James M. |title=Japanese etiquette today : a guide to business & social customs |date=1994 |publisher=C.E. Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.
==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{Cite journal |last=Hisao |first=Maye |date=1997 |title=Theorizing About the Origins of the Tokonoma |journal=Chanoyu Quarterly |issue=86 |pages=22–39 |issn=0009-1537 |oclc=4044546}}
==External links== * {{Commons category-inline}}

[[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese home]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]
}}

==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:توكونوما]][[be:Токанама]][[ca:Tokonoma]][[commons:Category:Tokonoma]][[cs:Tokonoma]][[de:Tokonoma]][[en:Tokonoma]][[eo:Tokonomo]][[es:Tokonoma]][[fa:تماشاگه]][[fr:Tokonoma]][[he:טוקונומה]][[id:Tokonoma]][[it:Tokonoma]][[ja:床の間]][[ko:도코노마]][[nl:Tokonoma]][[pl:Tokonoma]][[pt:Tokonoma]][[ru:Токонома]][[sl:Tokonoma]][[tr:Tokonoma]][[uk:Токонома]][[vi:Tokonoma]][[zh:床之間]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:توكونوما]][[be:Токанама]][[ca:Tokonoma]][[commons:Category:Tokonoma]][[cs:Tokonoma]][[de:Tokonoma]][[en:Tokonoma]][[eo:Tokonomo]][[es:Tokonoma]][[fa:تماشاگه]][[fr:Tokonoma]][[he:טוקונומה]][[id:Tokonoma]][[it:Tokonoma]][[ja:床の間]][[ko:도코노마]][[nl:Tokonoma]][[pl:Tokonoma]][[pt:Tokonoma]][[ru:Токонома]][[sl:Tokonoma]][[tr:Tokonoma]][[uk:Токонома]][[vi:Tokonoma]][[zh:床之間]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:توكونوما]][[be:Токанама]][[ca:Tokonoma]][[sn:commons:Category:Tokonoma]][[cs:Tokonoma]][[de:Tokonoma]][[en:Tokonoma]][[eo:Tokonomo]][[es:Tokonoma]][[fa:تماشاگه]][[fr:Tokonoma]][[he:טוקונומה]][[id:Tokonoma]][[it:Tokonoma]][[ja:床の間]][[ko:도코노마]][[nl:Tokonoma]][[pl:Tokonoma]][[pt:Tokonoma]][[ru:Токонома]][[sl:Tokonoma]][[tr:Tokonoma]][[uk:Токонома]][[vi:Tokonoma]][[zh:床之間]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q738760]] Wikidata name: tokonoma [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Alcove in Japanese reception rooms}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Kannonin Tottori16s4470.jpg|thumb|A {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} with a [[:en:Hanging scroll|Hanging scroll]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement]] [[File:Tokonoma (床の間).jpg|thumb|Detailed view of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and aspects of a Japanese room]] [[File:Murotsu Museum of Folklore08s5s4272.jpg|thumb|View from the side of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}]] [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto29s5s4200.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Tokonoma}} at [[Tenryū-ji]]]]
A {{nihongo||床の間|'''tokonoma'''}},<ref>''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'', Kenkyusha Limited, {{ISBN|4-7674-2015-6}}</ref> or simply {{nihongo||床|'''toko'''}},<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for ''tokonoma.''</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu. Iguchi Kaisen, et al., supv. eds. (Kyoto: Tankosha, 1986 10th ed.) entry for ''Toko.''</ref> is a recessed space in a Japanese-style reception room, in which items for artistic appreciation are displayed. In English, a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} could be called an [[:en:Alcove (architecture)|alcove]].
==History== There are two theories about the predecessor of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}: the first is that it derives from the room structure of the {{transliteration|ja|shinden-zukuri}}, which flourished in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) and declined in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1336–1573); the second is that it derives from the room structure of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] monasteries in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] (1185–1333). In the room of the monastery, there was a board called {{nihongo||押板|oshiita}} which displayed Buddhist altar fittings such as candlesticks, incense burners and vases. On the wall behind {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} was a hanging scroll with a Buddhist theme. The second theory is that the {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} and the back wall developed into a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]]}}-style {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} in the Muromachi period.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20210411010010/https://wafujyutaku.jp/archives/alcove-cat/seishinsei Tokonoma.] Shinken press.</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu, entry for ''Toko''</ref>
In {{transliteration|ja|shoin-zukuri}}, an architectural style developed in the Muromachi period, {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} came to be used as room decoration, and the owner of the house sat in front of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} decorated with various things to meet guests. However, in the case of important guests, the householder, in deference to them, had them sit in front of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/save/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BA%8A%E3%81%AE%E9%96%93-105027 Tokonoma.] Kotobank</ref>
==Characteristics== The items typically displayed in a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} are calligraphic or pictorial [[:en:Hanging scroll|scrolls]] and an {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement. [[:en:Bonsai|Bonsai]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Okimono|Okimono]]}} are also common—although traditionally, bonsai were not considered worthy for a place of such respect. The {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and its contents are essential elements of traditional Japanese interior decoration. The [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] {{transliteration|ja|toko}} (床) literally means "floor" or "bed"; {{transliteration|ja|ma}} (間) means "space" or "room". When seating guests in a Japanese-style room, the correct [[:en:Japanese etiquette|etiquette]] is to seat the most important guest closest to the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} as this is in the location furthest from the entrance, a location called the {{transliteration|ja|kamiza}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Vardaman |first1=James M. |title=Japanese etiquette today : a guide to business & social customs |date=1994 |publisher=C.E. Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.
==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{Cite journal |last=Hisao |first=Maye |date=1997 |title=Theorizing About the Origins of the Tokonoma |journal=Chanoyu Quarterly |issue=86 |pages=22–39 |issn=0009-1537 |oclc=4044546}}

In the room of the monastery, there was a board called {{nihongo||押板|oshiita}} which displayed Buddhist altar fittings such as candlesticks, incense burners and vases. On the wall behind {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} was a hanging scroll with a Buddhist theme. The second theory is that the {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} and the back wall developed into a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]]}}-style {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} in the Muromachi period.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20210411010010/https://wafujyutaku.jp/archives/alcove-cat/seishinsei Tokonoma.] Shinken press.</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu, entry for ''Toko''</ref>
In {{transliteration|ja|shoin-zukuri}}, an architectural style developed in the Muromachi period, {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} came to be used as room decoration, and the owner of the house sat in front of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} decorated with various things to meet guests. However, in the case of important guests, the householder, in deference to them, had them sit in front of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/save/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BA%8A%E3%81%AE%E9%96%93-105027 Tokonoma.] Kotobank</ref>
==Characteristics== The items typically displayed in a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} are calligraphic or pictorial [[:en:Hanging scroll|scrolls]] and an {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement. [[:en:Bonsai|Bonsai]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Okimono|Okimono]]}} are also common—although traditionally, bonsai were not considered worthy for a place of such respect. The {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and its contents are essential elements of traditional Japanese interior decoration. The [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] {{transliteration|ja|toko}} (床) literally means "floor" or "bed"; {{transliteration|ja|ma}} (間) means "space" or "room". When seating guests in a Japanese-style room, the correct [[:en:Japanese etiquette|etiquette]] is to seat the most important guest closest to the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} as this is in the location furthest from the entrance, a location called the {{transliteration|ja|kamiza}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Vardaman |first1=James M. |title=Japanese etiquette today : a guide to business & social customs |date=1994 |publisher=C.E. Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.
==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{Cite journal |last=Hisao |first=Maye |date=1997 |title=Theorizing About the Origins of the Tokonoma |journal=Chanoyu Quarterly |issue=86 |pages=22–39 |issn=0009-1537 |oclc=4044546}}

A {{nihongo||床の間|'''tokonoma'''}},<ref>''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'', Kenkyusha Limited, {{ISBN|4-7674-2015-6}}</ref> or simply {{nihongo||床|'''toko'''}},<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for ''tokonoma.''</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu. Iguchi Kaisen, et al., supv. eds. (Kyoto: Tankosha, 1986 10th ed.) entry for ''Toko.''</ref> is a recessed space in a Japanese-style reception room, in which items for artistic appreciation are displayed. In English, a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} could be called an [[:en:Alcove (architecture)|alcove]].
==History== There are two theories about the predecessor of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}: the first is that it derives from the room structure of the {{transliteration|ja|shinden-zukuri}}, which flourished in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) and declined in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1336–1573); the second is that it derives from the room structure of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] monasteries in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] (1185–1333). In the room of the monastery, there was a board called {{nihongo||押板|oshiita}} which displayed Buddhist altar fittings such as candlesticks, incense burners and vases. On the wall behind {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} was a hanging scroll with a Buddhist theme. The second theory is that the {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} and the back wall developed into a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]]}}-style {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} in the Muromachi period.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20210411010010/https://wafujyutaku.jp/archives/alcove-cat/seishinsei Tokonoma.] Shinken press.</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu, entry for ''Toko''</ref>
In {{transliteration|ja|shoin-zukuri}}, an architectural style developed in the Muromachi period, {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} came to be used as room decoration, and the owner of the house sat in front of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} decorated with various things to meet guests. However, in the case of important guests, the householder, in deference to them, had them sit in front of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/save/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BA%8A%E3%81%AE%E9%96%93-105027 Tokonoma.] Kotobank</ref>
==Characteristics== The items typically displayed in a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} are calligraphic or pictorial [[:en:Hanging scroll|scrolls]] and an {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement. [[:en:Bonsai|Bonsai]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Okimono|Okimono]]}} are also common—although traditionally, bonsai were not considered worthy for a place of such respect. The {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and its contents are essential elements of traditional Japanese interior decoration. The [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] {{transliteration|ja|toko}} (床) literally means "floor" or "bed"; {{transliteration|ja|ma}} (間) means "space" or "room". When seating guests in a Japanese-style room, the correct [[:en:Japanese etiquette|etiquette]] is to seat the most important guest closest to the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} as this is in the location furthest from the entrance, a location called the {{transliteration|ja|kamiza}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Vardaman |first1=James M. |title=Japanese etiquette today : a guide to business & social customs |date=1994 |publisher=C.E. Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:توكونوما]][[be:Токанама]][[ca:Tokonoma]][[co:commons:Category:Tokonoma]][[cs:Tokonoma]][[de:Tokonoma]][[en:Tokonoma]][[eo:Tokonomo]][[es:Tokonoma]][[fa:تماشاگه]][[fr:Tokonoma]][[he:טוקונומה]][[id:Tokonoma]][[it:Tokonoma]][[ja:床の間]][[ko:도코노마]][[nl:Tokonoma]][[pl:Tokonoma]][[pt:Tokonoma]][[ru:Токонома]][[sl:Tokonoma]][[tr:Tokonoma]][[uk:Токонома]][[vi:Tokonoma]][[zh:床之間]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:توكونوما]][[be:Токанама]][[ca:Tokonoma]][[co:commons:Category:Tokonoma]][[cs:Tokonoma]][[de:Tokonoma]][[en:Tokonoma]][[eo:Tokonomo]][[es:Tokonoma]][[fa:تماشاگه]][[fr:Tokonoma]][[he:טוקונומה]][[id:Tokonoma]][[it:Tokonoma]][[ja:床の間]][[ko:도코노마]][[nl:Tokonoma]][[pl:Tokonoma]][[pt:Tokonoma]][[ru:Токонома]][[sl:Tokonoma]][[tr:Tokonoma]][[uk:Токонома]][[vi:Tokonoma]][[zh:床之間]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:توكونوما]][[be:Токанама]][[ca:Tokonoma]][[sn:commons:Category:Tokonoma]][[cs:Tokonoma]][[de:Tokonoma]][[en:Tokonoma]][[eo:Tokonomo]][[es:Tokonoma]][[fa:تماشاگه]][[fr:Tokonoma]][[he:טוקונומה]][[id:Tokonoma]][[it:Tokonoma]][[ja:床の間]][[ko:도코노마]][[nl:Tokonoma]][[pl:Tokonoma]][[pt:Tokonoma]][[ru:Токонома]][[sl:Tokonoma]][[tr:Tokonoma]][[uk:Токонома]][[vi:Tokonoma]][[zh:床之間]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q738760]] Wikidata name: tokonoma [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Alcove in Japanese reception rooms}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Kannonin Tottori16s4470.jpg|thumb|A {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} with a [[:en:Hanging scroll|Hanging scroll]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement]] [[File:Tokonoma (床の間).jpg|thumb|Detailed view of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and aspects of a Japanese room]] [[File:Murotsu Museum of Folklore08s5s4272.jpg|thumb|View from the side of a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}]] [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto29s5s4200.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Tokonoma}} at [[Tenryū-ji]]]]
A {{nihongo||床の間|'''tokonoma'''}},<ref>''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'', Kenkyusha Limited, {{ISBN|4-7674-2015-6}}</ref> or simply {{nihongo||床|'''toko'''}},<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for ''tokonoma.''</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu. Iguchi Kaisen, et al., supv. eds. (Kyoto: Tankosha, 1986 10th ed.) entry for ''Toko.''</ref> is a recessed space in a Japanese-style reception room, in which items for artistic appreciation are displayed. In English, a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} could be called an [[:en:Alcove (architecture)|alcove]].
==History== There are two theories about the predecessor of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}: the first is that it derives from the room structure of the {{transliteration|ja|shinden-zukuri}}, which flourished in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) and declined in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1336–1573); the second is that it derives from the room structure of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] monasteries in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] (1185–1333). In the room of the monastery, there was a board called {{nihongo||押板|oshiita}} which displayed Buddhist altar fittings such as candlesticks, incense burners and vases. On the wall behind {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} was a hanging scroll with a Buddhist theme. The second theory is that the {{transliteration|ja|oshiita}} and the back wall developed into a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]]}}-style {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} in the Muromachi period.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20210411010010/https://wafujyutaku.jp/archives/alcove-cat/seishinsei Tokonoma.] Shinken press.</ref><ref>''Genshoku Chadō Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Chanoyu, entry for ''Toko''</ref>
In {{transliteration|ja|shoin-zukuri}}, an architectural style developed in the Muromachi period, {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} came to be used as room decoration, and the owner of the house sat in front of {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} decorated with various things to meet guests. However, in the case of important guests, the householder, in deference to them, had them sit in front of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/save/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BA%8A%E3%81%AE%E9%96%93-105027 Tokonoma.] Kotobank</ref>
==Characteristics== The items typically displayed in a {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} are calligraphic or pictorial [[:en:Hanging scroll|scrolls]] and an {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ikebana|Ikebana]]}} flower arrangement. [[:en:Bonsai|Bonsai]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Okimono|Okimono]]}} are also common—although traditionally, bonsai were not considered worthy for a place of such respect. The {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} and its contents are essential elements of traditional Japanese interior decoration. The [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] {{transliteration|ja|toko}} (床) literally means "floor" or "bed"; {{transliteration|ja|ma}} (間) means "space" or "room". When seating guests in a Japanese-style room, the correct [[:en:Japanese etiquette|etiquette]] is to seat the most important guest closest to the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} as this is in the location furthest from the entrance, a location called the {{transliteration|ja|kamiza}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Vardaman |first1=James M. |title=Japanese etiquette today : a guide to business & social customs |date=1994 |publisher=C.E. Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.
==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{Cite journal |last=Hisao |first=Maye |date=1997 |title=Theorizing About the Origins of the Tokonoma |journal=Chanoyu Quarterly |issue=86 |pages=22–39 |issn=0009-1537 |oclc=4044546}}
==External links== * {{Commons category-inline}}
{{Japanese architectural elements}}]]

The [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] {{transliteration|ja|toko}} (床) literally means "floor" or "bed"; {{transliteration|ja|ma}} (間) means "space" or "room". Tuttle |location=Rutland, Vt. |isbn=1462902391 |edition=1st}}</ref> Stepping within it is strictly forbidden, except to change the display, when a strict etiquette must be followed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-01-30 |title=What are the three rules in Washitsu? |url=https://japaneseroom.myshopify.com/blogs/the-guidance-of-washitsu/what-are-the-three-rules-in-washitsu |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Kai Japanese Room |language=en}}</ref>
The pillar on one side of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}, called {{nihongo||床柱|toko-bashira}}, is usually made of wood, specially prepared for the purpose. It can range from a seemingly raw trunk with bark still attached, to a square piece of heart wood with very straight grain. The choice of {{transliteration|ja|toko-bashira}} determines the level of formality for the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}}.
American architect [[FRank Lloyd Wright]] was influenced by Japanese architecture. He translated the meaning of the {{transliteration|ja|tokonoma}} into its Western counterpart: the [[:en:Fireplace|Fireplace]].<ref>Nute, Kevin (1993). ''FRank Lloyd Wright and Japan''. London: Chapman & Hall. p. 61</ref> This gesture became more of a ceremonial core in his architecture.
==See also== * [[:en:Fireplace mantel|Fireplace mantel]] * [[:en:Higashiyama culture|Higashiyama culture]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== * {{Cite journal |last=Hisao |first=Maye |date=1997 |title=Theorizing About the Origins of the Tokonoma |journal=Chanoyu Quarterly |issue=86 |pages=22–39 |issn=0009-1537 |oclc=4044546}}
